sentences.text: Yeah, but before we move to the ghetto, I want to tell you.
Because this wasn't so overnight, the whole story to take over the city and invading Poland.
But when the German moved into Lodz, they started discriminating against the Jews.
And they grabbed Jews in the street.
First, they didn't have-- they recognized from looks and they grabbed people to-- to work in the street, to clean the street, to carry stones, to carry water.
I don't know.
It doesn't-- didn't happen to me.
Later came out to wear a--
Armband.
And so I remember I was so affected with it, I didn't go out for a week from the house because I felt so degraded and humiliated that I didn't-- what could I do?
I didn't go out.
I said, I cannot do that.
But I finally had to go out.
And the tragedy was that everybody was afraid, especially men.
They grabbed men to do work and to beat them up for nothing or for whatever the reason was.
It moved too fast.
And the people came injured.
And that time, we're not yet organized medical help.
Maybe was-- I don't remember.
I wasn't involved in this kind of work.
And later came out-- we had to wear a star, a David star, on the front and on the back.
Because on the front you could cover eventually.
And the back should be recognized too.
So there's another insult which again took me time to digest it and to make peace with that.
But this what I wanted to say-- and they arrested people.
And especially, they arrested the people who were on top, the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]-- Community.
--the Jewish community, the top people who were the-- or they assigned people.
And two days later, they killed them.
So there had to be others and people that are hiding.
They wanted-- they didn't want.
It was very terrible, and it was getting cold and shortage of food-- no food at all, no supplies.
I really cannot tell you exactly how we survived.
I don't remember how it was.
I remember I brought a piece of bread from before they closed the clinic they gave me.
They somehow had the bakery and everybody got a piece of bread.
And me as a Jew, I was the only there when I still worked.
And they gave me a piece of bread to take home.
And this was the end.
I never saw anybody anymore.
So it was tragic.
Because the fact-- and the arresting and the killing and the beating up.
I saw the people, and I heard more than I saw.
Because I wasn't the whole city.
The city was big.
So later when we started moving to the ghetto was again, oh yes, so you wanted to-- the ghetto.
Maybe if I recall more about the city where beating, arresting, and killing, justice was still not organizing and the star and the armband.
Huh?
I have here a star.
You want to see it?
Later-- oh yeah, here on the-- I have a star somebody gave me after the war.
So we moved to the ghetto.
It was tragic because people were carrying on their back-- somebody who had the means, had some transportation to push.
Or somebody had-- there was already no cars.
People left with cars.
Whoever had a horse, a buggy for [INAUDIBLE], I don't know what was going on really.
And children crying and hungry and the mother carrying the children and then carrying stupid things which not important but didn't know what to do and what not to take.
So finally came the time when we moved.
So we moved, so we got-- and I don't remem-- and my brother worked to make the hospital ready.
And I started already working because people-- there was already a clinic--
--already, the temporary.
It changed everything.
So we were busy from the morning until the evening.
There was no time for your reflections and thinking of justice, of morale, of ethics.
We had to do things.
That's all.
We have no time to make your own opinion.
No.
My brother, when he was before the war broke out, had a technician who made for him all the plans because he was an engineer, he were.
And he was a non-Jew-- a Russian, by the way.
And it sounded to be a nice young man.
And in the ghetto, my brother contacted him through somebody who went to the city and to-- he never responded.
They said to answer I don't know him.
Nobody ever.
And my brother studied in Prague with some who were from Lodz-- never heard from anybody.
I didn't have any Gentile-- but he had.
He started in Prague one-- I remember the name.
I never heard from anybody, No.
But they couldn't maybe either.
They couldn't-- wasn't so easy.
Because we were hermetically closed.
Nobody could get in.
Nobody could get out.
There were some who went to work in the city.
So they checked them in and out.
They smuggled sometimes.
So they paid with their lives if they caught.
But did they smuggle a few potatoes?
I couldn't tell you.
I wasn't involved in that.
Who was checking the people?
The German.
At that time, maybe it was not yet Jewish police.
But later was Jewish, but has to be ordered.
So the population hated the police.
But I say that it has to be order.
And anybody who has a little power is hated.
And maybe they abuse the power.
I wouldn't say.
I don't know.
But I say under the circumstances, it's hard to blame.
Because if the person who hated would be in this position maybe would do the same thing.
I don't know.
I am lucky in my life that I didn't have to abuse and to do anything.
I did my job and to be to do the right thing.
I thought I couldn't do the wrong thing.
I did.
I could not go, but I had to go.
But I say because they were such hatred to the police, I don't know.
They abuse.
They came to catch the children, let's say, when there was action, we call it.
So if he wouldn't do it, the German would come to do it.
How you do it is also important.
History will judge everything.
And the Judenalteste who was in the ghetto, [NON-ENGLISH], they all-- he was the-- he went to his head too.
He was an old man.
They assigned him.
He wasn't by vote.
So he considered himself the king.
And he thought he is a king.
And he abuse.
He hits people.
He killed people.
He send out to the Germans people whom he didn't like.
But I still say, and I said in my previous video, that history will judge.
Because under unnormal conditions, it's hard to judge what is right, what is wrong.
What?
Oh no, what food?
It was such a shortage of food and was cold.
They gave us a supply of potatoes, let's say, for a month.
The Germans.
There was-- and this went through the Jewish hands.
They get the food and they divide it.
So also there probably were abuses-- the closest people.
The closer got more.
But there was-- we got a certain supply for a month.
I don't remember.
So we-- let's say potatoes.
The people-- they said not to eat it up because you had-- so it froze.
It got rotten.
We have to throw it out, the potatoes, which we didn't eat up.
And if we would eat up in the first week, you wouldn't give them.
So later was that people was begging the hospitals to give supply of the peeling of the potatoes.
And they wash the peeling-- which was already from rotten potatoes, by the way.
And they made pancakes from this.
They made pancakes from not coffee, but [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know.
How do you call it?
Chicory.
Chicory.
And I once wanted to eat.
It didn't go through my throat.
Didn't go.
So people tried to do what they could.
And everybody was hungry.
Not in the beginning, but later, sure was begging.
But who could give?
And in the beginning, people worked, you know, by selling clothes if somebody had exchanging-- I wouldn't say help people.
But maybe have money.
But exchanging-- so what's the smart way to sell a bread for a potato or a potato for bread?
It was hard to give an advice what is right, what is wrong.
Oh, what to do?
So in the beginning, people walked around, and you could see emaciated, emaciated more and more.
And I had once heard two men talk.
They said, I look like a Muselmann.
Because we called the very skinny a Muselmann.
Man.
They said, I don't look like a Musel-- it was a self-defense mechanism.
They both looked like Muselmann.
And finally, people died later in the streets, were laying-- hundreds of people walking.
There was no time of stop.
Yeah, they took them.
Where, I don't-- yeah, the Jewish burial.
They collected.
I?
I was busy from the morning to work-- first of all, I belonged to the category of younger doctors.
So we had assignments where the younger could do.
So first of all, I worked at the clinic for my specialty, my children.
Later, I had to schedule everybody-- wasn't the only one-- who worked in the emergency room.
So these were called days and night, middle of the night, they came.
They called.
There was a telephone, yes.
Here is a case there.
So we had a horse and buggy and go.
And the ghetto was divided in two parts-- the Lodz ghetto.
And between one part and the other was a bridge.
Why did they do it?
Because the two parts, the tram passed by.
And over there, the Poles, the real Poles, the [INAUDIBLE] so they had to be separated from us.
So we were completely divided.
And there were signs not to trespass because here is danger of epidemic and [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], epidemics.
And if a Pole would want to go in would kill be just the same.
And if a Jew wanted to get out, they were killed.
And there was plenty of German soldiers.
So now we, the doctors, had to go from one part of the city to the other.
So they open special gates, and they let us go.
But some German didn't know who's who.
So many of them shoot.
So later came out that each doctor wore a special hat.
I have here a book.
Oh, I don't have it here.
I gave to-- no, I have it Where they-- with the red [INAUDIBLE] here so the Germans should recognize that this is sanitary because they help the sanitarium, too.
So this was a little like a safety.
So we took transportation to take a patient from the house to a hospital, to a proper hospital.
We have to pass the non-Jewish section just to cross the street.
So with this hat served us.
And we worked very hard because everybody was sick.
There wasn't a non-sick person.
When I came to see a child-- so they opened the resorts, places where people worked.
And this is the-- not because we wanted.
We really collaborated with the war machine because the resorts saved the life of the ghetto because the people were making uniforms, shoes, boots, hats, gloves, whatever for the army.
And this was you get another soup if you worked.
And one soup was regularly, if it was.
So I came to see a child, an infant, in bed-- no parents, nobody.
The door was opened.
And I took off the covers.
So steam came out because it's so cold.
The whole wall was like a Christmas tree with stars of snow--
--in the house.
And in the bed was a baby.
And there was no milk.
So one pediatrician, she was the chief, she found out a way to make an artificial formula for the child.
Now, how long did that last?
They died.
So later came in the ghetto, people wanted badly to get to be admitted to the hospital for at least for a few days so there was a good bath and a warm, clean linen bed and a good food.
Because the hospital still supplied.
So one day, somebody called me and said, don't come today to the clinic.
So I was surprised.
And so what happened?
During the night, the German meanwhile came and took all from the hospital.
The supplies?
No, the patients, the patients.
It's unexpected-- some who were already not so sick maybe do-- the way out.
But they took everybody.
So somebody called me.
My brother-in-law.
I gave him the job.
My husband's the one I married.
He called me.
Don't come today.
Because he wanted to avoid me this aggravation that I will find out.
So this latest-- what I want to say.
People worked in the resorts.
It was very hard work.
And the Germany have to be a supply made because the German who took care of the whole ghetto, he himself, he saved his life.
He didn't have to go to the front, [NON-ENGLISH].
No, I never saw him, no.
And so the resorts was what I want to say, the hospital.
And all of a sudden, all of the sudden, there was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
They came.
They will take children.
So all parents have to come into the pediatrician to make them older.
You know, you can make from an eight-year-old that is 15 years old.
So whatever we did, we didn't do-- my colleague, a classmate, I found out later had two children.
And she said, children are going to be taken.
So the day when they were supposed to come, she put them between two walls in the wall to hide them.
Meanwhile, they took them away, the parents.
And later, all the houses were from wood was the slums.
These people steal, removed everything for a little heat, found two skeletons, the two children which she put over there.
She thought that they will save the children.
Nobody knew that over there are two children.
This was such shocks and to live here.
I remember when there were the big [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
They took my mother to go.
So I had another--
Appel and we had to be downstairs in the back yard-- if there wasn't, the street.
And some were hiding here.
Than they found them right away.
And my mother, we were honest to say-- go down, it's down.
They took her under then.
I was in the shock.
So a colleague of mine went over to a German.
Somehow, he had luck with him.
He said, you know, this woman is the mother of her doctor and maybe you let her go.
So he says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
So I saved my mother until the end when the ghetto was liquidated, until we went to Auschwitz.
But this is the-- now, during the times of the ghetto, start coming transports from abroad, which always at night so we shouldn't see it probably.
And in the morning, we found out new people from Berlin, from Czechoslovakia, from Prague, a boss of my brother's where when he was in Prague, he didn't have the right to work.
But probably he tried to help him to do something-- happened to be a Jew.
And he came with two daughters.
But he and my brother was already considered a big shot because he was a somebody in the ghetto.
Found him maybe an apartment a little better and maybe give him advice.
They did not know no Polish, you know.
Czech is similar.
It isn't the same.
So we went through different-- and these they brought from Germany or from other country, they were dying like flies.
We, all the trouble came gradually.
And we were used to trouble, and I didn't mention-- What were they dying of?
Malnutrition?
Everything-- cold, malnutrition, shock, deprivation, no apartment.
They came with hats, with high heels.
They didn't know where they are going, and they come to such a misery.
We were walking cadavers.
They thought that they are on another planet-- imagine so.
And the language and everything, there I got some patient too.
So they had no resistance at all.
And we had gradually built up resistance to sickness, resistance to malnutrition.
They couldn't so, they were dying like flies.
And they laid in the streets?
Yeah, they laid in the street.
And they were collected.
You saw it probably on television or worse.
In Lodz they didn't take because we were different.
I tell you the truth.
I never asked them.
Because I knew.
What would I ask?
They'd throw them out from that apartment like they threw us.
But the German, they didn't like too much the Polish Jews, the German.
So they considered that we were dirty and we were not educated.
But they are different.
But they didn't know that.
For a month, they were worse than we because we took it gradually.
But they couldn't, you understand?
You have to understand who we are.
We were not educated, poor, because the government didn't let you be anything else like a poor worker, a shoe maker, or a [NON-ENGLISH] or a tailor.
But you couldn't.
From the ghetto, or when they brought them to the ghetto?
We never knew where we are going.
When I was--
They told different versions.
There were notes in the trains, this and that.
People-- let's say they say if you go, you get a loaf of bread and a pound of sugar.
So people went.
They-- but they assume-- we didn't know.
No.
There were suicides in the beginning in this city and later in the ghetto too.
The more sophisticated, they committed suicide.
The religious wouldn't do that.
Probably there were maybe some very lucky.
But it was impossible from the Lodz ghetto.
Because we were hermetically closed.
I tell you, I didn't know about it.
But after the war I met somebody in Sweden, and later we will come to that, who was together with my brother in Auschwitz.
And he developed a [NON-ENGLISH] a skin condition.
And he right away was sick.
And a German did something to him.
And he lift his hand against the German.
He shot them on the spot.
But this same man who was with him from our hometown told me that they were together in the underground in Lodz.
I never knew about it, and I imagine that my brother didn't want me to be involved in case they catch me-- that if I don't know, I will not talk.
But if they catch me, I might talk.
He didn't know.
So I didn't know anything.
Yeah.
Yeah, we had also an order for the German to feed us a little better.
We should survive and we should do the job.

So besides the supply of food which we got, we got an extra [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], I don't know what to say-- an extra, let's say, for each doctor and the family so that the doctor should be able to work.
A ration, yeah-- a supplement, I would call it.
It wasn't too much, but it's still-- everything counted.
Yeah, sure.
He got also-- because he also was an asset for the German.
They knew him because the few which had something to do with the war machine, to say-- because they need the hospital and everything.
Yeah, now I remind myself-- in the ghetto, I was called my brother to the KRIPO-- that's Kriminalpolizei-- that somebody reported to them-- they talked to me that I have diamonds.
[NON-ENGLISH] And I should give it.
And before, I had a little golden bracelet, a little-- so I gave it to a friend who knew somebody from the KRIPO to tell them that this I have and that's all.
But later, when they took me to the real interrogation, I said, I don't know.
My father is dead, or it's 1933.
We never were very rich people and I only came here-- I had to pass examinations in the beginning.
And I am only a beginner.
I don't have anything.
They didn't touch me.
But I want to say I was never beaten by a German.
It means I am-- maybe other doctors also not.
But I was lucky.
On my own skin, I didn't feel it.
But when I was in the KRIPO and he interrogated me-- so he wanted to catch me, which I said the truth-- I didn't have to-- that they asked me that I am married.
I said, yes, I married two months before the war and my husband is in the army.
Where he is, I don't know.
So he said, if you tell us-- if you don't have an a diamond, who has diamonds?
I should report.
So we will tell you where your husband is.
So at that time, the one said-- and I looked at him.
And I said, how can he know?
He doesn't even know the name.
So I only looked at him.
So one said to the other, look at that.
She looks at you, and she had a fresh look-- and fresh and bleak.
So I was happy.
At least I showed him I had a freshened look, yeah.
Because-- That was the only defense you had.
He tells me-- he will tell me and I are going to report I didn't know.
And I wouldn't report.
But he wanted to trick me in.
Yeah.
No, no, inside the ghetto.
No, no.
My brother was called at the same time.
And he heard from outside, they beat-- they always to the emergency room.
They brought people from the KRIPO.
And we called it [NON-ENGLISH] because it was inflammation of those hard-- like a board and red and bleeding because they beat up.

So when my brother told me-- because it wasn't with me-- that when they interrogated him and he also said he doesn't know, he said if you don't talk, you-- it will be with you what you hear over there.
Over there is your [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
This they knew to say.
This is--
The angel of death.
But they didn't beat him either up, and not me.
Sure, the food condition-- people died and people prayed already to be taken wherever-- to change out, whatever, to make an end.
And the underground must have been something.
If I don't know, it doesn't mean it wasn't.
But this man told me that my brother was involved.
Yes or no, I don't know.
Yeah, we had a show.
We had a concert to give a little spiritual lift, emotional lift.
They played--
Yeah, for the children, yeah.
Yeah, yeah, schools and children.
And they even made-- in the periphery, they made like a little resort, rest resort, for recuperating of people who were of some importance.
They didn't want they should die.
So they went for the week for better food and better warm in the house and so and so.
I remember I-- sometimes I couldn't sleep full night.
I was stupid, too, at that time.
I was hungry and I was cold.
So I had the gas range and water.
I could take a half bottle and put on my feet.
I didn't think of it.
So I was cold and hungry both.
So at nights, I couldn't sleep.
All right.
What did your mother do the whole day?
She-- the food that they brought, she cooked.
She-- she worked in the resort too.
She knitted something.
So for the soup-- yeah, that's-- she did.
The additional soup in the [INAUDIBLE].
Good-- she knew to do it.
More.
And when I was work, out to work, I never knew when I come back whether I find my brother or I find my mother.
So alone--
No, I'm not so sentimental in this way.
And what will I make open big with wounds?
Nothing-- that's all.
We live like that, like--
From hour to hour, from day to day.
That's all.
I suffered additional.
Because, you know, this trauma for me, I got married to a man who was the man for me and all of a sudden after two months disappeared.
And I remember the one word to two words which I said to him before he left.
I will be waiting for you all the time.
And two days later, I was out from this place.
But I got letters from him.
Yeah.
In the ghetto, yeah.
He was released-- he was a prisoner of war.
But later, they got rid of him.
They sent him to the periphery of Poland, more to the east side.
And he knew the city, they found me somehow-- they found me.
And I got from him letters.
And I remember there was a woman.
I found out that in the same city where he was had a sister of hers, and the husband was a doctor.
So I met this woman.
I said, you know, I will help you here.
And you write to your sister.
She should help.
He was a doctor over there-- healthwise or other advice, to let them sit with them.
And they paid us in the ghetto.
So I helped this woman, and they helped my husband over there so that I knew a little bit.
